rationale: The citing paper uses Ehrich and Irwin (2005) for a specific empirical premise: consumers may fail to request ethical product information even though they would use it if available. It then explicitly pivots from that result to its own question about what happens when ethical information is already available, focusing on include versus exclude consideration-set formation. This is more than a passing background mention because the cited finding helps motivate the paper's core problem setup and contribution framing.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Ehrich and Irwin (2005) by moving from willful ignorance in information request to a new decision-context mechanism, testing how response mode (excluding vs including options) changes ethical attribute weighting when information is available.
